records.text: Composition-with-Anthropic-skills decision: Dendrite skills compose with Anthropic's native .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md format rather than competing with it. Native skills are the always-loaded floor (use for universally-relevant project orientation and core conventions). Dendrite skills are the dynamic, recall-scored layer on top (use for skills scoped to specific work patterns; emerge from repeated memories). The installer-shipped .claude/skills/dendrite-wiki/SKILL.md instructs the agent on how to discover Dendrite skills via wiki_context.skills and wiki_skill_load. This avoids being squashed by future Anthropic skill registry features and positions Dendrite as infrastructure those skills read from.

records.text: For dynamic indicators on VitePress nav links (e.g. notification counts on `Inbox`/`Review Board`), use Vue Teleport from a host component mounted in `nav-bar-content-after`. Pattern in `docs/.vitepress/theme/components/InboxNavBadge.vue`: (1) keep host component in the slot to own SSE/polling lifecycle; (2) on mount, querySelectorAll matching link elements (`a.VPNavBarMenuLink, a.VPNavScreenMenuLink` to cover BOTH the desktop nav and the mobile screen menu — they use different VPLink subclasses); (3) Teleport a `<span>` badge into each matched link; (4) attach a MutationObserver to `.VPNav` (NOT `.VPNavBar` — the mobile screen menu lives outside `.VPNavBar`) to refresh targets when VitePress re-renders the menu, but use a reference-equality check on the matched-list to skip no-op updates so the badge teleport (which itself mutates the link) doesn't loop. Avoid hardcoding base path: filter by `href` ending with `/wiki/...` so any `vitepress base` config works. Bonus: this approach naturally drops the standalone badge UI — the indicator now sits directly on the link the user needs to click, which is better UX (one visual cue, not two).

The in-browser editor + retro themes + AI Mermaid chart pipeline are end-to-end working including verified-with-real-Ollama generation, inline ✎ chart editing, and `binder:export` CLI. 432 tests passing, build clean. Three things explicitly deferred and tracked in this handoff (NOT bugs — backlog by choice): (1) delete-page UI from the editor, (2) auto-promotion of hand-authored Mermaid charts to marker'd ones on first edit, (3) the F5 Reveal Codes split-pane preview (currently a stub button that toggles a ref but doesn't show anything). - F5 Reveal Codes split-pane was scoped in the R5 plan but dropped during the slice. If we add it: split the editor body horizontally, left pane stays as CodeMirror source, right pane renders the markdown preview live via markdown-it (already a dep from R6 binder export). Handoff summary: Redesigned the Benchmark Report page (docs/.vitepress/theme/components/BenchmarkReport.vue) to match the MaintenanceReviewBoard's minimal-tactical aesthetic and added rich self-explaining hover tooltips to every data point. Two passes shipped this session: (1) visual rebuild — replaced gradient/shadow card panels with hairline-bordered sections, a stripe-tape "BENCHMARK" header, italic Times-serif `*Local*Benchmark` hero, a 4-stat strip with thin colored ticks, and 4-column trend rows (label · value · 32px sparkline · delta) with hairline dividers and a leading 2px tone-tick; (2) tooltip pass — every trend row, hero stat, status pill, hero-window dt/dd, recall strip cell, maintenance strip cell, and section header now carries a multi-line `title` describing what it measures, direction-of-good, latest value, and per-snapshot history series. Hover affordance via `cursor: help` and subtle text-1 3% background tint. New CSS tokens scoped under `.bm-report` (bm-color-improve/warn/orientation/health/recall/context/ops + bm-hairline). Script logic essentially unchanged — TrendMetric gained a required `description` field, three lookup tables (headlineMetricInfo, recallStripInfo, maintenanceStripInfo) describe the static stats, helpers (metricTooltip, headlineTooltip, stripCellTooltip, metricSeriesText, toneDirectionText) build the multi-line title strings; trendPath gained an optional height arg; shortCommit + statusLabel computeds added. tsc clean; verified end-to-end via agent-browser at 1400×2400 with DOM query confirming 15 trend rows + 4 stats + 10 strip cells all carry tooltips.

Next steps:
- Consider replacing native `title` tooltips with a custom component (e.g., a small popover that appears immediately on hover) if the OS-level ~1s delay feels too slow — review board pattern uses native title so consistency is fine for now.
- Dogfood the redesign for a session or two and watch whether the operator still has to ask 'what does X mean' — if a specific tooltip is unclear, refine that one description in the metric arrays / lookup tables.
- Evaluate whether the same tooltip pattern should be ported to MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue — its hero stat tiles and work-item rows could benefit from the same self-explanatory affordance.
- If recall benchmark history grows past ~12 snapshots, the truncated history line in metricSeriesText (`first → … → last 6`) may want re-tuning — current truncation triggers above 8 entries.

Open questions:
- Native title attribute multi-line rendering varies by browser — confirmed working in Chrome/Edge via DOM query, but Firefox/Safari behavior wasn't tested. Worth a manual cross-browser pass before assuming the tooltip pattern is universal.
- Should the section-header tooltip also include a short example (e.g., what a 'good' Wiki Health row looks like) — or is the per-row tooltip enough?
- The shadow-mode semantic recall strip currently has no tooltips on its three cells (Probes with cosine, Avg cosine across candidates, Avg cosine deterministic top-1). Worth adding for parity, or leave it sparse since it's a kill-switch metric most readers shouldn't care about?

records.text:
Handoff summary: Review Board row removal now sweeps off to the right before the gap closes, instead of disappearing instantly. The fix is purely CSS in MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue: (1) replaced the existing subtle `.rb-item-leave-*` rules with a staged sweep — translateX(112%) over 520ms with an aggressive ease-in tail, opacity fades 80ms in, and max-height/margin/padding/border-width collapse is delayed ~280ms so the row visibly leaves before the gap closes; (2) added `overflow-x: hidden` to `.work-list` so the off-screen translate doesn't trigger a page-level horizontal scrollbar; (3) **critical follow-up** — the first attempt silently no-op'd because `.work-item.just-completed` (specificity 0,2,0) sets a `transition` shorthand and stays on the row during the leave (the just-completed timer clears at 5.5s, long after the leave finishes), overriding the bare-class `.rb-item-leave-active` (0,1,0). Bumped leave/enter/move/leave-from/leave-to/enter-from selectors to `.work-item.rb-item-*` to tie specificity, and source order does the rest. Reduced-motion override at line ~2826 still uses bare-class selectors with `!important` and was left untouched (intentional — !important beats specificity, so reduced-motion users still get instant removal).

User reported "doesn't seem to work" after the first round; the specificity fix landed in a follow-up. User did not confirm whether the second attempt now visibly sweeps — they may still need a hard refresh (Ctrl+Shift+R) to bust HMR/CSS cache. No code-level testing was done; this is CSS-only and Vue's compiler accepts it without complaint.

Captured two memories: a skill on TransitionGroup leave staging (sweep first, then collapse the gap), and a warning on the specificity gotcha when sibling state classes set the `transition` shorthand during the leave.

Next steps:
- Confirm with the user that the sweep actually animates after a hard refresh — if it still snaps, inspect the leaving `<li>` in DevTools during the leave and check what `transition` resolves to (should be the long shorthand from .work-item.rb-item-leave-active). If the resolved transition is wrong, look for further specificity competitors not yet identified.
- Consider auditing other state classes in MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue that might set a `transition` shorthand and clash with TransitionGroup classes (search for `\.work-item\.[a-z-]+\s*\{` blocks that contain `transition:`). The just-completed clash was the obvious one — there could be more lurking for tone/category variants.
- If the sweep direction or speed feels off in user testing, tweak `.work-item.rb-item-leave-active` transform duration (currently 520ms) and easing (currently cubic-bezier(0.55, 0.06, 0.68, 0.19)). Decreasing duration to ~440ms or pushing translateX to 130% are easy levers.
- Run `npm run benchmark:snapshot -- --label session-end` if any further work happens in this area; this session was visual-only and no benchmark was captured at start either.

Open questions:
- Did the sweep land for the user after the specificity fix, or does it still feel like the item is teleporting? They reported the first attempt didn't work but did not return after the fix landed.
- Is the 520ms sweep duration the right length? It's intentionally longer than the previous 420ms to make the motion legible, but in fast workflows where the operator is clicking through many items rapidly, the row may still be mid-flight when they click the next action. Worth observing in real dogfood usage.
- Should the sweep direction be parameterized by purpose? Promote actions could sweep up-and-right (graduating), Quiet could sweep down (dismissing), Reconcile could sweep left (back into the wiki). Currently all three sweep right uniformly. Probably not worth doing unless the metaphor really helps comprehension — added complexity for marginal payoff.

records.text: Memory Trails design — three deterministic patterns ported from dendrite-mcp predecessor after audit revealed which bio-inspired patterns actually worked vs which silently failed: (1) Edge reinforcement + lazy evaporation: new project_memory_edges SQLite table with (from_kind, from_id, to_kind, to_id, edge_type, weight, last_reinforced_at, created_at, evaporation_rate). Reinforce on wiki_context recall hits (+0.05), wiki_skill_load (+0.10). LAZY on-demand evaporation at read time: effective_weight = weight * (1 - rate)^hours_since_reinforced. This sidesteps the predecessor's tokio-scheduler design (we're stdio MCP, no background process). Use as recall ranking bonus surfaced as 'reinforced Nx over last Nd' in reasons[]. Per-edge-type rates: query→memory 0.005/hr, memory→file 0.001/hr, page→page 0.0005/hr, attached_skill 0.003/hr. (2) LRU+TTL cache on wiki_context: 256 entries, 30-min TTL, invalidate on any wiki_write/memory_remember. Pure latency win. (3) Jaccard drift lint: tokenize wiki page front-matter intent vs last N project-log entries; Jaccard distance > 0.5 raises maintenance-review finding 'page drift suspected'. Works without embeddings or LLM. Predecessor's mycelial growth (embedding-based) is explicitly NOT ported because it ran broken for months in dendrite-mcp (smoking gun: store.rs:15167 comment 'name was a bug that silently disabled this pass for months').

records.text: Pattern for adding a new LanguageExtractor (Python is the worked example; Rust/Go follow the same shape): (1) detect() must require BOTH a project-signal file AND a usable toolchain — never claim a project whose extract() will throw at runtime. Memoize the toolchain lookup in module scope so detect() doesn't pay version-check overhead on every call. (2) For shell-out extractors, embed the helper script as a string constant and pipe it via stdin (`spawn(cmd, ['-', source-path])` for Python, equivalent for Ruby/etc.). NEVER pass the script via `-c` because Windows cmd.exe caps total command line at ~8KB. (3) The helper should emit JSON in the language-agnostic ApiFileReference shape directly — let the orchestrator do path resolution and slug derivation, the helper just produces symbols. (4) walk() should default to language-conventional excludes (Python: tests/, .venv/, build/, __pycache__/, site-packages/). Set respectInternalConvention:false for languages whose privacy convention isn't @internal JSDoc — the language-specific is_public() check should live in extract(). (5) Symbol-kind heuristics need at least one negative test case (e.g., DEFAULT_NAME != type-alias) — easy to write a heuristic that's right on PascalCase but wrong on SCREAMING_CASE. (6) Tests must skip cleanly when the toolchain isn't available on CI/dev — use `node:test`'s `{ skip: '...' }` option, not silent passing. Memoize-cache reset (`resetXyzInterpreterCache()`) is needed for tests that simulate "toolchain disappears."

records.text: Predecessor dendrite-mcp had a mycelial_growth_pass (store.rs:15148) that did O(n²) cosine similarity on embeddings to discover memory-to-memory connections. The audit revealed this pass ran BROKEN for months (the code looked for a 'memory_embeddings' table but the actual table was 'vec_items') and nobody noticed because there was no observable success metric. The lesson: bio-inspired patterns sound elegant in design docs but if they have no success metric they may be silently producing nothing — and you'll never know. When porting any pattern with statistical/probabilistic behavior, instrument it from day one (log empty-result rate, compare against a baseline, surface in benchmark snapshot). Specifically for this project: do NOT port mycelial growth or any embedding-dependent recall enhancement until we have (a) embeddings infrastructure and (b) a success metric in the recall benchmark that would catch silent failure.

records.text: Review Board design direction: the operator should not have to triage memories manually. Target shape is (1) a "Growing" tab showing active memories with no finding yet, (2) an "Auto-clean" button that delegates to the local LLM, which emits a structured {memoryId, verb, reason, confidence} per memory (verbs: archive, keep-and-watch, add-source, merge, promote-to-wiki, promote-to-skill, rephrase), (3) a summary panel that lets the operator audit each decision after the run. Confidence in the LLM comes from auditability + reversibility, not blind trust. Prerequisites: an unarchive/restoreMemory API (none exists today — archive is one-way) and a stable decision schema with per-run batch IDs so an entire auto-clean run is revertible. The whole feature fits inside the existing review→inbox→action architecture as a new judgment layer between "findings" and "actions"; no structural rewrite needed.

records.text: Skill enforcement decision: agents drift on tool discipline (documented in mem_7d531792). The skill discovery flow can't depend on agents remembering to call wiki_context. The fix is hook-injected enforcement: a UserPromptSubmit hook fires wiki_context automatically on every user prompt and injects matched skill summaries; a PreToolUse hook on Edit/Write fires a quick scope match against the file path/language and injects matching skill summaries as a system reminder. Hooks ship in the installer under .github/hooks/ alongside session-start, session-handoff, and benchmark hooks.

records.text: Skills matching approach decision: deterministic-only for v1 free tier, NO local LLM dependency. Rationale from auditing the predecessor dendrite-mcp project: (1) the LLM brought misfire rate from ~30% to ~5% but the bulk of the win came from deterministic guard-rails (language hard-filter + level/recency demotion), not the LLM; (2) our two-phase fetch design is fundamentally different — wiki_context returns skill summaries, the frontier coding agent (Claude/Cursor/etc) picks which to load via wiki_skill_load, so the frontier model already does semantic reranking and we don't need a second LLM; (3) dendritemcp-lessons explicitly lists 'Heavy Background Model Dependency' as a pattern to avoid. Borrowed deterministic guard-rails: scope hard-filters before scoring (conservative — missing scope dim keeps skill in candidates), recency demotion so historical high-recall skills don't dominate, token bigram bonuses for multi-word phrases. Optional Ollama/embedding reranker can be added post-S7 gated on real precision metrics if needed.

records.text: Skills-as-memory architecture decision: skills are a new memory kind ('skill') that extends the existing memory record with a scope schema (filePatterns, frameworks, languages, taskKeywords, matchMode). The frontier coding agent (Claude/Cursor/etc) — not a local LLM — picks which skills to use via a two-phase fetch: (1) wiki_context returns skill *summaries* matching the task scope; (2) the agent calls wiki_skill_load(id) for the ones it picks. This mirrors the existing wiki_search → wiki_read pattern and avoids requiring a local LLM. Free tier (no gating). Build phases S1–S7 in skills-as-memory.md.

records.text: Team-tier architecture decision: Team tier centers on a hosted node (Supabase + thin Node service initially) holding the canonical wiki/memory/skill store, plus a steward agent on that node that handles all cross-engineer merges. Sync is local-first: writes happen locally first, queue, then push in background. The steward classifies every action into high/medium/low confidence — high lands directly, medium lands with an auto-revert window, low queues for human review with the steward's recommendation attached. The reviewer always has final say. Recommended starting model for the steward is Claude API with prompt-cached system prompt (cost is fine at team scale, quality matters because errors create reviewer fatigue). Build phases T5–T8 in team-tier-architecture.md.

records.text: Team-tier reporting model decision: pull-only, not push. The Team dashboard is a Next.js app managers open when they want to know status. No auto-post to Slack/email/etc. Rationale: (1) push requires connector infra (HubSpot/Slack/etc) which is explicitly out of scope per operator design call; (2) pull is cheaper to build (no webhook infra, no rate limits, no outbound auth flows); (3) an always-current dashboard solves the 'managers want status' problem without spamming chat. records.text: Two specific JSDoc/TSDoc gotchas that bit during the file-level docstring pass for the API reference generator: (1) Any literal `*/` sequence inside a JSDoc body terminates the block. This includes glob patterns like `**/*.ts` (which contains `**/*` → `*/` in the middle) — they look fine in source but the parser sees the comment end mid-pattern. Rewrite to avoid the literal `*/` substring, or break the pattern with whitespace. (2) When the API reference renderer extracts file-level JSDoc bodies into markdown pages, multi-line backtick spans containing `<word>` placeholders (like `dendrite-wiki skill:import\n<path>`) trip VitePress's Vue compiler — Vue parses the angle brackets as unclosed tags before markdown processing kicks in. Inline single-line backtick spans like `<id>` are usually fine, but anything that wraps to a new line breaks. Safe alternatives: rewrite as descriptive prose ("the subcommand takes a memory id"), or use square-bracket placeholders. Also: when a walker uses `/@internal\b/` to detect `@internal`-tagged files, anchor it to tag-position (`^[ \t]*\*?[ \t]*@internal\b` with /m) so prose mentions in docstrings don't cause modules to self-skip.

records.text: VitePress parses every markdown page as a Vue SFC, so any literal `<word>` substring in a page body trips the Vue tag parser with "Element is missing end tag" and breaks `npm run docs:build`. This is especially dangerous in auto-generated wiki pages that emit operator-supplied content into markdown — `docs/wiki/maintenance-inbox.md` is generated by `src/wiki/maintenance-inbox.ts` from project-local memory bodies, and a single memory containing `.github/agents/<name>.agent.md` was enough to break the whole docs build. The defense lives at the markdown sink, not the input: `escapeMarkdownForVue()` in `src/wiki/maintenance-inbox.ts` HTML-escapes `<` and `>` to `<`/`>` before emitting `finding.summary` into the `####` heading and `record.text` into the blockquote. The escape preserves backticks, code blocks, and other markdown formatting; it only neutralizes the Vue tag parser. When adding any new emit point in the inbox generator (or any other generator that writes user-supplied content into a `.md` file under `docs/wiki/`), apply `escapeMarkdownForVue` to the user-supplied portion. Test/maintenance-inbox.test.ts has a regression test ("escapes angle brackets in memory summary and body so VitePress can render the page") asserting both the heading and blockquote escape correctly.

records.text: When a Node test spawns the CLI via `child_process.execFile` with a Windows `.cmd` shim (e.g., `npx.cmd`, the platform-specific `npxCommand`), pass `shell: true` in the options. Without it, `execFile` returns `EINVAL` because Windows cannot directly execute `.cmd` files without a shell to dispatch them. The MCP SDK's StdioClientTransport handles this internally so existing MCP tests work, but a plain execFile call does not. `shell: true` is harmless on POSIX where `npx` is a real executable. Saw this on the api-cli tests; spent ~5 minutes recognizing EINVAL is the canonical Windows-cmd-shim error.

records.text:
When a TransitionGroup leave animation feels like an item is "just disappearing" rather than physically being removed, look at whether the slide and the height collapse are running on the same timeline. If they are, the row's gap closes while it's still in place, so the eye reads it as a fade — not a sweep.

The fix: stage them. Make the transform run first (longer duration, bigger distance — translateX(110%+) is enough to clearly leave the visible area), and add a `transition-delay` to max-height/margin/padding so the gap only starts closing after the row has visibly moved. Use an aggressive ease-in for the slide (cubic-bezier(0.55, 0.06, 0.68, 0.19)-ish) so it accelerates as it goes — this reads as "swept", not "drifted".

Why: subtle translateX values (e.g., 0.75rem) plus simultaneous height collapse compress into ~400ms of overlapping motion that the operator perceives as a single fade-out. Operators reported items "just disappearing" even though there was a 360ms transition. Two staggered phases (sweep, then collapse) each get their own perceptual moment.

How to apply: any list with TransitionGroup leave where you want the user to register affirmative removal. Also remember to add `overflow-x: hidden` (or `clip`) to the list container so the off-screen translate doesn't trigger a page-level horizontal scrollbar, and keep the existing `prefers-reduced-motion` override pointing at the leave-active rule so reduced-motion users still get instant removal.

items.summary: Memory has no supporting sources: When adding/changing a Vue `<TransitionGroup>` leave (or enter) transition, check whether any *other* class on the sa...
items.reason: No supporting sources are attached, so the memory cannot yet be traced back to code, commands, wiki pages, or decisions.

records.text:
When adding/changing a Vue `<TransitionGroup>` leave (or enter) transition, check whether any *other* class on the same element sets the `transition` shorthand. The TransitionGroup leave classes (`.foo-leave-active`, etc.) have specificity 0,1,0; if a sibling state class like `.work-item.just-completed` (0,2,0) is also on the element during the leave AND sets `transition: <any-property> ...`, that rule wins and silently *overrides* the entire leave transition shorthand. The transform/opacity/max-height properties then SNAP to their leave-to values instead of animating, and the row appears to disappear instantly even though the leave-to styles are correct.

Concrete example from this project: MaintenanceReviewBoard had `.work-item.just-completed { transition: border-color 320ms ease, box-shadow 320ms ease; }`. The `just-completed` class stays on the row during the leave (the visual-feedback timer clears it at 5.5s, well after the leave finishes), so it overrode `.rb-item-leave-active`'s transition. The fix is to bump the leave selectors to match the state class's specificity — e.g. `.work-item.rb-item-leave-active` — so they tie on specificity and win on source order.

How to apply: any time you write or debug a TransitionGroup transition, audit OTHER class rules that may apply to the same element during the transition. If any of them set the `transition` shorthand, raise the leave/enter rule's specificity (double-class selector is the cleanest) so it wins. Symptom to recognize: leave-to styles are correct on inspection but the element snaps instead of animating.

records.text: When building a TypeScript declaration→markdown renderer, two specific polish targets matter for output quality: (1) For interfaces, just call `printNode(node)` and strip the leading `export ` modifier. Don't try to render `interface Foo` headers without the body — readers care about the member shape, which IS the interface. The TS printer handles indentation and member ordering cleanly. (2) For function parameter signatures, build the rendered text manually instead of using `printNode(parameter)`. Default values like `= {}` are implementation detail and add visual noise. Build as `${dotDotDot}${name}${question}${typeAnnotation}` to keep just the contractual parts: rest token, identifier, optional marker, type. Saw this when dogfooding the API reference generator on this repo's 37-module src/ tree — the unfiltered output was cluttered, the polished output is professional. Discovered during Phase A6 of the api-reference-roadmap.

records.text: When documenting a flow, system, request path, or state machine in a wiki page, consider calling `wiki_insert_chart` to add a Mermaid diagram. The tool validates the source, anchors by heading (so the chart stays attached to the right section even after edits), and writes idempotently — calling it twice with the same source is a no-op. Anchor pattern: `anchorKind: "after-heading"` + `anchorHeading: "Request Flow"` (or whatever section header the diagram illustrates) is the right default. Diagram types Mermaid supports well from text descriptions: flowchart for code paths and decision trees, sequenceDiagram for request/response flows between services, stateDiagram-v2 for lifecycle/state machines, classDiagram for domain models, erDiagram for database schemas. A diagram is worth adding when prose alone makes the reader trace through entities and arrows mentally — if you find yourself writing "X calls Y which then writes to Z and notifies W", that paragraph is a diagram in disguise. Don't add charts for decorative reasons (a "system overview" diagram that just boxes-and-arrows the same things the prose already names is noise). Call `wiki_replace_chart` with the original `chartId` (printed in the inserted block's marker comment, e.g., `<!-- chart:auto-flowchart-7e9f1a3 -->`) when iterating; do not delete + reinsert. The `caption` parameter renders as italic `*Figure: ...*` below the chart and is a good place to call out one specific thing the diagram clarifies that the surrounding prose doesn't.

The wider `dist` value drags in `dist/docs/.vitepress/*` and `dist/scripts/*` because tsconfig.json includes those source directories — but at runtime nothing in the published package needs them: the bin entries are `dist/src/index.js` and `dist/src/cli.js`, no src/* code references compiled scripts/ or docs/.vitepress/ outputs (verified — no `__dirname`/`fileURLToPath`/`import.meta.url` lookups in src/, and install.ts embeds all seeded content as strings rather than copying from the package install location). records.text: When using prefers-reduced-motion to disable Vue/CSS animations, do NOT just set `animation: none !important` — that leaves any element whose visible state depends on the animation's `forwards` final-frame stuck at its initial-frame state (e.g. an SVG checkmark with stroke-dashoffset:22 stays invisible because the draw-in animation never runs to set offset:0). Instead, in the reduced-motion media query EXPLICITLY apply the resolved final values (`stroke-dashoffset: 0 !important; opacity: 1 !important; transform: none !important`) so reduced-motion users still see the completed visual, just without the bloom. Verified concretely on MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue's .rb-completion__seal-ring/check and .rb-completion__label after agent-browser eval revealed the seal's computed dashoffset never moved off 100.5px on a system with prefers-reduced-motion: reduce. Pattern applies to any draw-in / fade-in / scale-in animation gated by a media query.

items.summary: Memory has no supporting sources: When walking TypeScript ASTs purely for declaration/JSDoc extraction (no type checking), prefer `ts.createSourceFile(...
items.reason: No supporting sources are attached, so the memory cannot yet be traced back to code, commands, wiki pages, or decisions.

records.text: When walking TypeScript ASTs purely for declaration/JSDoc extraction (no type checking), prefer `ts.createSourceFile(path, text, target, setParentNodes=true, ts.ScriptKind.TS)` over `ts.createProgram(...).getSourceFile()`. Two reasons learned the hard way during the API extractor implementation: (1) Program-loaded SourceFiles do NOT have parent pointers set, so anything that walks parents — `ts.getJSDocCommentsAndTags(node)`, `node.getText()`, `node.getSourceFile()`, the TypeScript printer's printNode — silently returns empty/undefined or throws. (2) JSDoc is still attached to declaration nodes via the `.jsDoc` property regardless of parent pointers, so direct `(node as { jsDoc?: ts.JSDoc[] }).jsDoc` access works without a Program. Take the LAST element of the `.jsDoc` array as the owning doc — file-level JSDoc gets attached to the first declaration too, so the first entry can be a leftover from leading file comments. Use `setParentNodes:true` only when you actually need parent traversal; you almost always do once you start using getText() or printer.

Two pieces are required: (1) `runActionViaBridge` accepts an `onAccepted` callback that fires the moment the bridge POST returns 200 (BEFORE the 1.4s completion-overlay hold and `refreshBoardData()`). (2) `reseedExpandedGroupsIfNeeded` MUST seed expand state ONCE on the first non-empty inbox snapshot and never reseed again. records.text: B3 (operator phrasebook + ritual-hook pattern matcher) shipped 2026-05-11. New module src/wiki/operator-phrasebook.ts exports OPERATOR_PHRASE_RULES (19 entries across 4 categories: durable-intent, scope-setting, session-boundary, reviewer-control), matchOperatorPhrases(prompt), and formatOperatorPhraseNudges(matches). New wiki page docs/wiki/operator-phrasebook.md documents the phrases-as-API contract with tables grouped by category and links to the source-of-truth module. WHY this matters: the agent's memory_remember quality is bounded by how much causal language the operator puts in the prompt; high-signal multi-word triggers like "from now on", "the reason we", "whenever you're editing", "wrapping up", "pin that", "promote that" are unambiguous enough that nudging the agent at UserPromptSubmit time has near-zero false-positive risk and lubricates the right post-action behavior. WHY multi-word patterns: "always" or "never" alone fire constantly on routine prose; phrases like "from now on" are rare enough that a hit reliably means a durable rule. Word-boundary regex applies to the right edge only — patterns starting with a content word (not preceded by other letters in practice) — so "from now once" does NOT match "from now on". MATCH ALGORITHM: lowercase the prompt, scan for each rule's substring, check the next character is non-alphanumeric (or EOS). Returns one match per rule. Multiple categories can fire on a single prompt; each surfaces independently. CLI WIRING: ritual:hook (Claude/Codex UserPromptSubmit) and ritual:cursor-hook (Cursor beforeMCPExecution) both read stdin JSON, extract payload.prompt if present, run matchOperatorPhrases, and append the formatted [DENDRITE OPERATOR PHRASEBOOK] block to the existing [DENDRITE RITUAL CHECKPOINT] additionalContext. When Cursor's payload omits prompt (the typical case), matchOperatorPhrases returns [] silently — no error, no nudge. Critical design decision: the hooks NEVER block — this is purely advisory. Tests at test/operator-phrasebook.test.ts (13 tests covering empty/missing prompt, every category, word-boundary, multi-category, case-insensitivity, casing preservation, routine-prose quiet path, format-output shape, vocabulary). records.text: B9 (sleep-cycle consolidation pass) shipped 2026-05-11. New module src/wiki/consolidate.ts orchestrates the three input streams (memory_review findings + auto-promote candidates + auto-archive candidates) into a single clustered report via union-find on shared anchors. ANCHOR DEFINITION: each finding's anchors are the union of its source record's relatedFiles + `page:<slug>` for each relatedPages entry + `tag:<name>` for each tag, all lowercased. Two findings end up in the same cluster when their anchor sets share at least one element (transitive via union-find — A shares with B, B shares with C → A/B/C all in one cluster). Findings with no anchors go to `orphans` so they aren't lost. Clusters sorted by descending size then lexicographic id for stability. WHY clustering by anchors not by kind: the operator's natural triage unit is "everything about this file/page" not "everything stale", so a single click should let them address all findings touching one area. CLI: `dendrite-wiki consolidate [--apply] [--max-clusters N]` — default dry-run prints the report; --apply orchestrates auto-promote + auto-archive sweeps under shared cap (default 10 split half-and-half between the two). THREE env vars required for full --apply: DENDRITE_AUTO_CONSOLIDATE=on (gate the orchestration), DENDRITE_AUTO_PROMOTE=on (for promote sub-sweep), DENDRITE_AUTO_ARCHIVE=on (for archive sub-sweep). WHY three gates not one: each sub-sweep already has its own opt-in semantics; collapsing them into one consolidate gate would surprise operators who set DENDRITE_AUTO_CONSOLIDATE expecting just clustering. Tests at test/consolidate.test.ts: 9 tests covering empty store, single-cluster, multi-cluster, orphans, maxClusters cap, descending-size sort + id tiebreak, env-var-gated apply refuse, toConsolidateFindings anchor extraction (file/page-prefixed/tag-prefixed), isAutoConsolidateEnabled env-var matching. NO MCP TOOL surface yet (CLI-only). The future surface can be `wiki_consolidate` if real usage shows chat-driven consolidation requests are common; until then the operator runs the CLI from a terminal or wires it into a cron / git hook. records.text:
Before committing changes that land ahead of an `npm publish` workflow on this repo, run `npm run check` locally end-to-end (wiki:refresh → build → tests → docs:build) — not just `npx tsx --test test/*.test.ts`. The full check is the same script CI runs pre-publish, and the focused test sweep can miss flakes that only surface under different timing.

Why: in 0.4.0-alpha.1, two synthesis tests in mcp-server.test.ts (bounded-proposal-synthesis at line 1156, agent-handoff-synthesis at line 1193) read problemFixtureRoot directly while two preceding apply tests mutate that same fixture in-place and rely on try/finally to restore. On a slow CI runner the spawned MCP server raced the finally restore, so the synthesis tests saw a mid-edit fixture (AGENTS.md temporarily short → 1 proposal instead of 2). Local Windows machine never reproduced; CI did. The user had to run the publish workflow once to find it. Pattern: tests that share a mutable fixture with apply tests are flaky in proportion to runner slowness — fix is to mkdtemp+fs.cp the fixture into a per-test temp dir, same pattern the memory tests already use.

How to apply: any time a commit chain is about to be pushed to a branch that triggers an `npm publish` or release workflow, run `npm run check` locally first. If `check` is green and only then commit + push. If a test in mcp-server.test.ts uses a shared mutable fixture (problemFixtureRoot or fixtureRoot) without an mkdtemp wrapper, treat it as a candidate for the isolation pattern even if it passes locally.

items.summary: Memory is incubating: Browser print preview is theme-independent: a `@media print` block in CSS fires when ANY user triggers print (Ctrl+P...
items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 0 times, never recalled; 2 days old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: Browser print preview is theme-independent: a `@media print` block in CSS fires when ANY user triggers print (Ctrl+P or window.print()) regardless of which retro theme they have active. So put print rules at the global level, NOT scoped to `html[data-dendrite-theme='X']` like screen-themed retro overrides. The screen-themed @media print scoping (e.g. `html[data-dendrite-theme='selectric'] .VPNav`) only fires when the operator was already in that theme — defeats the goal of "one-click pretty print from any theme." Use bare selectors inside `@media print { ... }`. Also: `window.print()` cannot bypass the browser's print dialog (no browser allows programmatic-print without user gesture), so the UX goal is to make the *preview that appears in that dialog* look right by default — don't try to skip the dialog. Set `@page { margin: 0.7in 0.6in 0.8in 0.6in }` for sensible margins that play well with default browser headers/footers. Verification limitation: agent-browser CLI's evaluate doesn't expose Playwright's `page.emulateMedia({media:'print'})` — to visually verify, the operator has to hit Ctrl+P in a real browser, OR you can launch Chrome with `--emulate-media=print` flag.

records.text: CodeMirror 6 inherits VitePress theme palette automatically when the editor is configured with `EditorView.theme({ '&': { color: 'var(--vp-c-text-1)', backgroundColor: 'transparent', fontFamily: 'var(--vp-font-family-mono, monospace)' }, ... })`. Pass `{ dark: false }` as the second argument so CM doesn't add its own `cm-theme-dark` class that would fight the parent CSS — the dark/light decision lives in the VitePress theme tokens, not in CodeMirror. Using `backgroundColor: 'transparent'` lets the modal's parent `var(--vp-c-bg)` show through, which means switching the dendrite retro theme (Amber/WordPerfect/Selectric) live re-styles the editor without touching CM internals. Required deps for a minimal markdown editor: `codemirror` (umbrella + basicSetup), `@codemirror/state`, `@codemirror/view`, `@codemirror/lang-markdown`, plus `@codemirror/commands` (defaultKeymap, history, historyKeymap), `@codemirror/language` (syntaxHighlighting, defaultHighlightStyle, bracketMatching, indentOnInput), `@codemirror/search` (searchKeymap). For read-only mode use BOTH `EditorState.readOnly.of(true)` AND `EditorView.editable.of(false)` — the first blocks programmatic transactions, the second blocks DOM contentEditable.

items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 0 times, never recalled; 2 days old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: Mermaid's Node-mode parser is unreliable for server-side validation. `mermaid.parse(source, { suppressErrors: true })` returns `false` for almost any non-trivial chart when there's no DOM, because Mermaid internally calls `DOMPurify.addHook` which fails without a browser environment. Charts that render perfectly in the browser get rejected by the parser. So if you need to validate Mermaid source server-side, DON'T just call mermaid.parse(); either install jsdom and polyfill `global.window`/`global.document` BEFORE importing mermaid (heavy but accurate), or use a heuristic validator that catches the actual LLM failure modes (keyword-on-first-line check, bracket balance for truncation detection, connection-operator presence for diagram types that need them — flowchart/sequence/state/class/er, with gantt/pie/journey/timeline exempt — and HTML script/iframe rejection for defense-in-depth on top of mermaid's `securityLevel: 'strict'`). The heuristic approach false-negatives some malformed input but never false-positives valid content; the rendering step in the browser catches the rest at display time, where errors are visible and recoverable.

items.summary: Memory is incubating: Modal responsive layouts at narrow widths: nested flex INSIDE a fixed-viewport grid is great for "always-visible" sti...
items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 0 times, never recalled; 2 days old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: Modal responsive layouts at narrow widths: nested flex INSIDE a fixed-viewport grid is great for "always-visible" sticky header/footer + scroll-only-the-middle pinning, but it's HOSTILE to content that varies in size at narrow widths. The failure mode: each grid row gets its allocated height, the card inside it has flex children (header, pills, summary, body), and the rigid non-flex children eat all the height before `flex: 1; min-height: 0` on the body can claim its share — body collapses to ~30px with a useless scrollbar. The fix: flip the pattern when the form factor changes. At narrow widths, switch the modal-panel itself to `height: auto; overflow-y: auto`, and set every internal scroll region (cards, text bodies, grids) to `flex: none; overflow: visible` so content flows naturally. Cap individual elements (e.g., `max-height: 18rem` on a code block) so one outlier doesn't dominate, but otherwise let the modal scroll like a mobile sheet. Trigger via container queries (preferred — `@container <name> (max-width: 760px)`) with a `@media (max-width: 900px)` fallback for browsers that don't support container queries (Chrome <105, Firefox <110, Safari <16). Don't try to keep a fixed-viewport modal at narrow widths — the math doesn't work and content gets clipped.

records.text: store.ts captures `process.cwd()` at module-load time (line 512: `const repoRoot = path.resolve(process.cwd());`), which means tests that need fixture-isolated execution must use ONE temp directory for all phases of work — not one per test. The existing benchmark.test.ts pattern works because it has exactly one test() block; if you copy that pattern with multiple test() blocks each creating their own mkdtemp + chdir + fs.rm cycle, the second test will fail with ENOENT inside listWikiPages because store.ts still holds the path of the deleted first temp dir. The fix: combine multi-phase fixture-dependent tests into a single test() block with phases inside it (see test/report-export.test.ts for the working pattern). The longer-term fix would be to refactor store.ts to lazy-resolve cwd per call, but that touches many call sites and changes module behavior.

records.text: Synaptic tagging slice (2026-05-06): when you need to up-weight observation clusters by session outcome, the contract is `src/wiki/session-outcome.ts`. classifySessionOutcomes(observations) returns Map<sessionId, SessionOutcome>; aggregateClusterTag(sessionIds, outcomes) returns ClusterSynapticTag with successSessionCount/errorSessionCount/inconclusiveSessionCount and a derived synapticTag ('verified-success' | 'likely-error' | 'inconclusive'). The verified-success rule is conservative: requires at least one verification command (npm test, pytest, cargo test, tsc, jest, git commit, etc — see VERIFICATION_COMMAND_PATTERNS) with outcome=ok, and the LAST verification in the session must not have ended in error. likely-error is assigned when the last verification failed OR when error observations exist with no successful verification at all. detectRawObservationClusters in src/wiki/raw-observations.ts now classifies once per inbox build (not per cluster) and sorts verified-success first via synapticTagSortPriority. The ClusterSynapticTag is surfaced in MaintenanceInboxSnapshot.observationClusters[].synapticTag and rendered as a Tag column badge in the inbox page. To extend with new verification commands, add to VERIFICATION_COMMAND_PATTERNS — they are case-insensitive substring regex matches against observation.target. To debug "why did this cluster get tagged X", call classifySessionOutcomes on the contributing sessions' observations and inspect each verdict's `reason` field.

records.text: The review board groups work items by PURPOSE (promote / reconcile / quiet), not by source kind (lint / memory / proposal). Purpose is derived from the primary action's kind via PURPOSE_BY_ACTION_KIND in MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue: apply-memory-promotion / draft-memory-promotion / promote-memory-to-skill / create-memory-from-cluster → promote; apply-proposal / edit-page-summary / insert-h1 / refresh-review-pages / read-* / check-* / rerun-* → reconcile; archive-memory / snooze-page-drift / archive-guidance-file → quiet. To add a new action kind, add it to PURPOSE_BY_ACTION_KIND or accept the default ('reconcile' fallback). Group order is FIXED via PURPOSE_ORDER = ['promote','reconcile','quiet'] — Promote always renders first because it's the most positive work; Quiet is at the bottom because it's housekeeping. Empty purpose groups are filtered out of groupedWorkItems so the board never renders a hollow "Quiet (0)" header. Per-item categoryLabel ("Memory · Promotion Ready", "Lint · Page Drift") is preserved as the eyebrow inside the verb-grouped list — within a Reconcile group the operator can still tell at a glance whether they're looking at a memory finding vs a lint vs a proposal. Hero stat tiles mirror the verbs (promote/reconcile/quiet/urgent) — old memory/lint/proposal counts and the totalCount tile were removed.

records.text: The review board has ONE interaction model: click row → unified item-detail modal. There are no inline action buttons on rows, no expanded-card details on the board, no separate confirmation dialogs. The modal (PromotionPreviewModal.vue, despite the legacy filename) accepts a discriminated `target` union of four kinds: memory-promotion / wiki-proposal / memory-promote-skill (each fetches a /preview/* endpoint and renders a diff or record-card body) and item-detail (no preview, renders rationale + per-source-type context body from a `context` prop the parent provides). EVERY variant shows the full action set as a labeled buttons panel near the bottom, with one-line descriptions per action ("Writes the memory text into the target wiki page and marks the memory superseded.", "Snooze 30 days — adds an entry that suppresses the lint signal", etc.). The preview-apply action emits 'apply' (parent uses skipConfirm + onAccepted glue); every other action emits 'runAction' for plain bridge dispatch. Board rows reduce to a single clickable button: eyebrow + title + subtitle + caret. The maintenance-inbox.md wiki page is collapsed to a thin redirect stub pointing at /review-board; the structured maintenance-inbox.json snapshot remains the data source. KNOWN REGRESSION: the inline AI-driven page-drift resolver (generate fix → approve/regenerate/snooze with multi-state flow per item) was on the row as an inline editor and is currently NOT in the modal — the operator can still snooze/insert-h1/read-page via the modal action panel, but the AI fix flow needs to be re-implemented inside the modal as a follow-up slice.

records.text: Trust-gated auto-promotion is operator-opt-in via DENDRITE_AUTO_PROMOTE=on. Three usage modes: (1) `dendrite-wiki memory:auto-promote --dry-run` previews candidates anytime regardless of env var — safe diagnostic. (2) `DENDRITE_AUTO_PROMOTE=on dendrite-wiki memory:auto-promote` applies one sweep manually. (3) `DENDRITE_AUTO_PROMOTE=on npm run wiki:refresh` runs the sweep automatically before regenerating docs — operator-set-and-forget pattern. The sweep NEVER fires from the per-action wiki refresh inside maintenance-runner — that path runs on every UI click and would create a feedback loop. The gate is intentionally STRICT (recall≥20, typed sources, target page exists, no contradictions, allowed-kinds excludes skill) — anything uncertain stays in the operator-review inbox. To extend: edit AutoPromoteCriteria defaults in src/wiki/auto-promote.ts. To loosen for a specific run, pass `criteria` to autoPromoteMemories programmatically. The maxPerSweep cap (default 10) protects against runaway churn. Auditability: all promotions still write to git-tracked files (target wiki page + docs/wiki/project-log.md), so `git diff` is the operator's review surface, not the maintenance inbox.

records.text: Visual aesthetic for the review board is "JRPG menu chic" — Stella Glow / Octopath Traveler / Triangle Strategy territory. Color discipline is the load-bearing rule: meaning lives on small accents (urgent ticks, per-purpose verb dashes, tone numbers) NOT on panel backgrounds. The board has zero filled cards — every section is delineated by a hairline divider (1px at 8-12% text-1 opacity), small-caps section labels (letter-spacing 0.18-0.22em, weight 600-700), and tabular-nums on every numeric value. Hero stat strip uses light-weight (300) numbers at 2rem with tiny colored tick marks (2px tall, 0.55rem wide) above them — no tile chrome at all. Group headers are section dividers: large light-weight verb (300, uppercase, slight letter-spacing), inline small-caps tagline, hairline below, accent dash to the left of the verb in the per-purpose color. Work-item rows are list entries: no border, no background, no rounded corners — only a bottom hairline between rows, hover gets a 3% bg wash and a 0.2rem padding-left shift on the summary text. Caret is `›` (single-character right chevron) at 1.05rem light weight, slides 2px right on hover. Diamond (✕ rotate 45deg) ornaments at the start/end of major hairlines for a small JRPG flourish. The modal is the only place the board allows itself a more structured surface — appropriate because the modal IS the focused decision surface.

records.text: VitePress cold-cache build flake: after deleting docs/.vitepress/cache and docs/.vitepress/dist, the next `npm run docs:build` can fail with `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'imports')` thrown from `resolvePageImports` in vitepress's SSR page resolver. Re-running the build immediately succeeds. The failure is not caused by the markdown changes — it's a vitepress SSR race during the first build after a cold cache. When `npm run check` fails on `docs:build` after edits, retry once before assuming a real regression.

items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 13 times, last recalled 0 days ago; 2 days old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: VitePress markdown files are processed as Vue templates, so any literal `</tag>` text inside a markdown file will fail Vue's HTML parser with "Invalid end tag" errors. This bit project-log.md when wiki_log entries from earlier in this session leaked tool-call closing syntax (literal `</entry>` and `</invoke>` strings) into the markdown body. The build error was opaque — `[vite:vue] docs/wiki/project-log.md (169:1408): Invalid end tag` with no clue that the cause was tool-call leakage. Defensive habits: (1) when calling wiki_log, only pass plain prose — never include tool-call XML or anything that looks like an HTML close tag; (2) when npm run docs:build fails with "Invalid end tag" on a wiki page, search the page for any `</word>` patterns that aren't real HTML; (3) consider adding a wiki_log validator that strips or refuses input containing `</[a-zA-Z]+>` patterns to prevent recurrence.

items.summary: Memory is incubating: When a VitePress theme component holds a composable result that contains nested Refs (like UsePageInboxResult { snaps...
items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 0 times, never recalled; 0 days old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: When a VitePress theme component holds a composable result that contains nested Refs (like UsePageInboxResult { snapshot: Ref<...>, loading: Ref<...> }) AND the component needs to reassign the whole result when an input changes (slug changes), use shallowRef NOT ref. The reason ref() fails here is that ref() auto-unwraps any nested Refs inside its value — so `inbox.value.snapshot` becomes the unwrapped value (not the ref), and the subsequent `.value` access crashes with "Cannot read properties of null (reading 'value')" because the initial null state has no .value property. Vue surfaces this as a generic "Unhandled error during execution of render function at <ComponentName>" warning with no further detail, which makes it surprisingly hard to diagnose. shallowRef preserves the nested refs as-is because the wrapping ref does not recursively unwrap, so `inbox.value.snapshot.value` works correctly at every state. Trade-off: ref()'s auto-unwrap is intended for the common case of "I want to access nested refs without writing .value", but it silently breaks the "I want to hold and pass refs around as a composable's return value" pattern.

items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 1 time, last recalled 0 days ago; 0 days old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: When a wiki_context call returns "result exceeds maximum allowed tokens" and falls back to disk-saved chunked reading, the culprit is almost always one of: (1) omittedPageReasons growing unbounded as the wiki accumulates pages — each entry carries the full match-reason string ("content mentions X; claim text matches Y; ..."); 96 entries × ~350 chars = ~34k chars of payload that exists only as a triage hint. (2) Full handoff/memory/skill body text embedded in the briefing when the briefing is supposed to be a *briefing* — full bodies are available via memory_recall (by id) and wiki_skill_load. The fix is bounded caps in buildWikiContext: maxOmittedPageReasons (default 12) + maxOmittedReasonChars (default 80), maxHandoffTextChars (1200), maxMemoryTextChars (600), maxSkillTextChars (800), with a `truncateForBriefing(text, max)` helper that appends "…" so the agent knows it was clipped. Trade-off: agents must call wiki_skill_load / memory_recall for full bodies when the truncated body isn't enough — which is already the documented contract (the briefing already says "call wiki_skill_load(id) for full content"). Measured against the dendrite-wiki-mcp dogfood repo: caps reduced payload 91k → 38k chars (58%) without losing any actionable information. The reason this matters: the briefing is the single hottest tool surface (one call per session start by ritual), and an overweight briefing makes session resumption slow and expensive without making it more useful.

records.text: When adding a new caller of findMaintenanceInboxAction (in src/wiki/maintenance-inbox.ts), the caller MUST load `reviewProjectMemories()` from src/wiki/memory-store.ts and pass `findings.memoryFindings` in the options argument. Without it, every memory action ID silently resolves to undefined and the bridge returns 404 unknown-maintenance-action. The review bridge had this bug latent for two roadmap phases (M3 hygiene + M4 promotion shipped, but the bridge wasn't updated to load memory findings). Tests at the unit level passed because they called findMaintenanceInboxAction directly with memoryFindings set; only an integration test at the bridge level would have caught it. The regression test at test/maintenance-inbox.test.ts now pins the contract: without memoryFindings the lookup MUST return undefined, with it MUST return the action.

records.text: When adding a new lint resolve-action to the maintenance review board, you must touch ALL of these surfaces or the action won't work end-to-end: (1) the kind/tool unions in MaintenanceInboxActionHint at src/wiki/maintenance-inbox.ts — both `kind` and `tool` are narrow string unions that need the new value added; (2) buildLintActions at the same file — unshift the new action so it appears before Read/Re-run and becomes the primary button; (3) buildLintActionId's actionKind union — this is a DIFFERENT narrow union that bites you at compile time only after step 2; (4) the dispatcher case in src/wiki/maintenance-actions.ts plus a new MaintenanceActionResultKind entry; (5) updatedPaths shape on the result object — used by maintenance-runner.ts:extractChangedPaths for audit trail and project-log emission; (6) requiresBridgeConfirmation in BOTH src/wiki/review-bridge.ts AND actionNeedsConfirmation in MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue (they must match — the bridge will 409 the request if the gates disagree); (7) loggedResultKinds in maintenance-runner.ts:buildProjectLogEntry decides which actions get an audit entry in docs/wiki/project-log.md. Order matters too: the lint action only renders as a primary button if it's available AND surfaces ahead of the read-wiki-page/rerun-lint pair via .unshift. Tests need the runMaintenanceActionIsolated helper because store.ts captures repoRoot at module-load via process.cwd(), so dynamic import in the same test process re-uses the runner's cwd, not the temp dir. Spawn a subprocess that chdir's first.

items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 1 time, last recalled 0 days ago; 0 days old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: When adding a new lint rule that scans page prose against memory content (the contradicts-shipped-memory pattern), the precision trade-off forces three design choices in order: (1) the matcher must capture an "object" noun phrase via regex capture group, not the whole sentence, because affirmative-keyword scoring against full sentences hits noise constantly; (2) require at least 2 distinct content-token overlap of length>=5 between the captured object and the affirming memory's text — fewer than that, generic words like "store" or "memory" hit every memory in the project; (3) ship a frontmatter opt-out directive (e.g., contradicts-shipped-memory: ignore) on day one because pages legitimately carry intentional negation as design language (privacy boundaries, product positioning like "No Local LLM Required", roadmap phases describing what is deliberately not yet implemented). Without the opt-out, the rule fires on intentional-negation pages and the operator either disables it or starts ignoring all findings — both kill the value. Pattern: detector returns ContradictionSignal[] from a pure function taking pageContent + memories[] + projectLogContent, the lint rule integration in store.ts loads listProjectMemories({includeArchived:true}).filter(status=active||superseded) once before the page loop, skips project-log itself (chronological log not a claims surface), and skips the rule when memories array is empty.

records.text: When building a new per-page Vue affordance that needs to reuse the maintenance-inbox apply path (e.g., per-page memory badge), the cleanest architecture is: (1) new src/wiki/page-inbox.ts as a pure projection over existing reviewProjectMemories + lintWikiPages results — emit the SAME maintenance-inbox actionId scheme `memory:<reviewKind>:<memoryIds>:apply-memory-promotion` so /actions/execute accepts the ids unchanged; (2) new GET bridge endpoint /__review-bridge/pages/inbox?slug=... that calls buildPageInboxSnapshot — read-only, no token gate change needed; (3) shared composable docs/.vitepress/theme/composables/usePageInbox.ts caches snapshot per-slug across components (badge + ghost) so they don't double-fetch; (4) badge in `#layout-bottom` slot (bottom-left to avoid the existing right-aligned Print/New/Edit stack), inline ghost cards in `#doc-after` slot so they render AT the end of the rendered markdown — exactly where memory_promote's appendPromotionBlock will land them. Avoid trying to reuse PromotionPreviewModal directly from a new mount point — it expects a parent owning runActionViaBridge with elaborate confirmation/onAccepted glue; a simpler inline apply call against /__review-bridge/execute is cleaner and preserves the audit story (the bridge writes through maintenance-runner.ts unchanged). Tests: store.ts captures wikiRoot at module init via process.cwd(), so per-test chdir patterns won't propagate to lintWikiPages — use one chdir + one import per test file with before/after hooks and rewrite memory store contents between tests.

records.text: When operator-supplied content needs to flow from the maintenance review board UI into a backend action, do NOT add a generic argumentOverrides map to the bridge — add a narrow typed scalar (or small typed object) for that one action's needs. Pattern: bridge POST body grows one new optional field (e.g., summaryDraft: string), runMaintenanceActionAndRefresh accepts it via RunMaintenanceActionOptions, executeMaintenanceAction pipes it through ExecuteMaintenanceActionOptions, and only the specific action's switch case consults it. Other actions ignore the field. Reasoning: a general overrides map would let an operator rewrite slug/path/days/etc. on any action, opening a path-traversal-style risk. The narrow field is impossible to misuse against unrelated actions because they don't read it. Vue side: runActionViaBridge took an argumentOverrides param shaped like { newFirstParagraph?: string } and serialized it as summaryDraft on the wire — keeps the frontend caller-friendly while the wire format stays narrow. Confirmation gate must mirror in TWO places: requiresBridgeConfirmation in src/wiki/review-bridge.ts AND actionNeedsConfirmation in MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue. They will silently disagree if you only update one (bridge 409s, UI doesn't show window.confirm), so always grep both when adding a new high-risk action kind.

records.text: When the operator asks you to "organize the wiki", "run the librarian", or words to that effect, the canonical workflow is: (1) call `wiki_librarian_audit` ONCE — it returns every open maintenance signal with pre-gathered evidence and a `recommendedAction` sentence per item, so you don't need to chain wiki_maintenance_inbox + memory_review + lintWikiPages + per-page reads yourself; (2) work down the items in the order the audit returns them (promotion-ready first because it's the most deterministic, then contradicts-shipped-memory, then page-drift, then claims/orphan lint); (3) execute each `recommendedAction` using the named tools — typically memory_promote with mode="apply" for promotions, wiki_read + wiki_write for prose rewrites; (4) for page-drift findings on roadmap/tracker-style pages, expect that even an accurate rewrite leaves Jaccard overlap below the 35% threshold because the project log inherently uses more concrete shipping vocabulary than any prose can match — the right close-out is to snooze via `wiki_execute_maintenance_action` with actionId `lint:page-drift:docs/wiki/<slug>.md:snooze-page-drift` (30-day quiet period); (5) at the end, summarize what changed so the operator can `git diff` the actual edits without rereading the audit. Every change still writes through the audited path (project-log entry + memory supersede + reversible file mutation), so the operator's safety story stays identical to manual editing.

records.text: When you need to decorate VitePress sidebar or nav links with dynamic per-link metadata (counts, badges, status indicators) and the default theme has no slot for it, the pragmatic pattern is a DOM-side-effect-only Vue component mounted in #layout-bottom: (1) component template renders nothing visible (`<span style="display:none">`) — its only job is mount lifecycle; (2) on mount, fetch the data and run a `decorate()` function that does `document.querySelectorAll('.VPSidebar a[href*="/wiki/"], .VPNav a[href*="/wiki/"], .VPLocalNav a[href*="/wiki/"]')` and appends a child span with the badge to each matching anchor; (3) a MutationObserver on document.body with childList+subtree fires `decorateSoon()` on debounce (~80ms) to handle VitePress re-rendering the sidebar on route change or expand/collapse — the debounce is essential because expanding a section fires many mutations in rapid succession and re-querying the whole sidebar on each one is wasteful; (4) styles for the injected class MUST be in a non-scoped `<style>` block (the global CSS) because the injected nodes live inside sibling components' DOM trees and scoped styles wouldn't apply to them. Use `pointer-events: none` on the badge so clicks pass through to the parent anchor. Forward-compat selector strategy: prefer specific VitePress classes but include `[href*="/wiki/"]` as a fallback so the decorator keeps working if VitePress renames a class in a future release.
